title: How are you using AI in trading?
author: TimelyJudge8679
selftext:
I’ve been thinking a lot about AI in trading lately and I’m curious how people are actually using it in practice.

Personally, I’ve been using AI mainly for backtesting, strategy evaluation, and generating trading signals. I’ve found it really useful for analyzing data faster, testing ideas, identifying patterns, and helping structure rule-based systems.

But I’m wondering how many people are actually letting AI execute trades fully autonomously versus just using it as a decision-support tool.

It seems like there’s a big difference between:

\- using AI to help evaluate setups and backtest ideas

  vs

\- giving AI full control over entries, exits, and risk management in live markets.

In theory, autonomous AI trading sounds powerful, but markets are dynamic and messy. Overfitting, regime shifts, liquidity changes, and unexpected news can all break systems quickly.

My current impression is that a lot of traders probably use AI more as an assistant rather than a completely hands-off trading system. Kind of like:

“AI helps process information and generate signals, but humans still manage the framework and risk.”

Curious where people here fall:

1. AI for research/backtesting only

2. AI-generated signals with manual execution

3. Semi-automated execution

4. Fully autonomous AI trading

What has actually worked for you?

comments.author: BuildwithPublic
comments.body:
Your instinct is right here- most serious traders use AI for a lot of mechanical stuff: scanning, regime filters, trade structuring, preflight checks. 

Full autonomy typically breaks down is regime shifts and unexpected news — no rules-based system has seen everything so it’s almost impossible. The setup that works is AI handles analysis and structure, you confirm execution. The infrastructure to do this at the retail level properly exists now in a way it didn’t two years ago - very much worth exploring if you haven’t already. Happy to chat

replies.author: TimelyJudge8679
replies.body:
This makes alot of sense. I am currently building out a data backed solution that I am thinking through the best way to layer AI into it. Right now the flow is this: 

1. Pre defined setup example is say an ORB 
2. AI confidence score that filters out weak setups
3. If setup passes confidence score, automatic entry with sl and tp levels placed into broker via an api
4. Trade monitoring and pre set stop loss adjustment rule 

Before even doing step one, back testing all stratagies using risk managment to verify potential edge. 

https://algobull.ai/proof

Let me know what you think. I'd love to chat more with you on this. I want to see where addittional value can be added. Feel free to dm me. 

I was playing around with a market regime classification system. But still experimenting.

replies.author: TH48
replies.body:
Nice maybe we should connect. 

Did build Gold, NASDAQ and two Dax ORBs and right now testing it on private and prop account

replies.author: TimelyJudge8679
replies.body: For sure, if you have a website dm me!

comments.author: Kindly_Ganache9027
comments.body:
I’m mostly in the “semi-automated” category. I use AI + automation more as infrastructure than a fully autonomous trader.

For example, I’ve automated an ORB scanner using n8n that tracks opening range levels, checks prices every 5 minutes, detects breakouts, and sends Telegram alerts in real time. I’ve also automated Zerodha login/token handling with TOTP + Telegram notifications before market open.

I do automate some execution workflows too, but I still keep risk management and strategy validation manual. Markets change too fast for fully hands-off systems.

What’s worked best for me is using AI/automation to reduce repetitive work, speed up analysis, and react faster — not blindly hand over all decisions.

comments.author: Opening-Berry-6041
comments.body: Hey man, your breakdown of AI in trading is super insightful, like you've really thought through the nuances of it, so I was wondering, have you ever encountered any AI models that can genuinely adapt to unpredictable market regime shifts without just overfitting to past data in a way that messes everything up?

comments.author: MoreView2660
comments.body:
Regarding your question, I'm really not sure how you would use AI for a fully autonomous AI trading system.

I personally tried it in the past using Claude and ChatGPT, but the limit I've found is : how do you trigger AI execution? I mean, each AI execution will cost tokens, so you can't trigger AI for each M1 candle close right?

You need some sort of algorithmic signal that "awakes" the AI agent at the right time, to validate the setup and execute the order.  
I had built a system where a pine script was detecting order blocks and would trigger the agent (with webhook) when price would revisit the OB. Based on automated screenshots of the charts sent with the webhook, the AI was in charge to validate the setup, and compute entry, SL and TP levels, and send the order to a MT5 EA script to execute the trade.

  
The system was not profitable because of multiple reasons:  
\* the webhook trigger was not always relevant (but honestly the pine script was doing a good job).  
\* the levels were not correct : SL was too tight, or TP was too far away.  
(a lesson I've learnt is that AI don't do math really well...)

I've struggled a long time on the prompt, but at the end, the issue I fell into was that the prompt had too many rules. The agent struggled applying each rule, whereas an EA in MT5 would have done a better job.  
Eventually, I stopped the development and completely went into another direction.  
  
Now I'm working on what you describe here: “AI helps process information and generate signals, but humans still manage the framework and risk”. I think it's a way better approach

I'm currently testing a new platform that you can use like TradingView, but with an AI assistant embedded. The model is completely different, and built on AI agentic concepts.  
Basically the AI assistant has direct access to the chart and technical indicators (OHLC, ICT zones, Market structure, EMA, RSI,...).  
Based on the trader request, the AI agent decides in full autonomy which information he needs : he can do multi-timeframes analysis, ICT, or any strategy you want him to use.  
When analysis is done, he can draw zones and levels directly on the charts, and can suggest setups with appropriate risk based on capital for money management. Setups are delivered in mini "card" format with an execute button.  
You can also connect the platform with MT5 and cTrader for direct trade execution.  
At the end, when you have the setup ready, and all the analysis, your only job is to "press the button" to confirm execution.

Honestly, I've been using it since a few weeks now, and results are really great, because "I" can wait for the right confirmation.

  
If you want to test it, access is free. I'm actually looking for feedbacks on the platform.

comments.author: Busy-Specialist7708
comments.body:
We use AI as a decision-support layer, not as a blind execution bot.

The biggest mistake people make is thinking the edge is “AI finds entries.” For us, entries are the last step. The real value is using AI to structure the process before execution even matters.

Our workflow is more like:

First, define the market environment.  
Then identify what is actually driving the move.  
Then compare strong vs weak currencies.  
Then narrow down the cleanest pair expression.  
Only after that does execution become relevant.

So AI helps with macro scanning, regime classification, event interpretation, backtesting, journaling, and pressure-testing ideas. But it does not get unlimited authority to just click buttons because a pattern appeared.

Markets change. Regimes shift. News can invalidate a setup fast. So full autonomy without a framework is dangerous. It is basically giving a fast machine permission to make bad decisions faster.

For us, AI is not the trader.  
AI is the analyst, memory bank, quant assistant, and process checker.

The human still owns the framework, risk, and final authority.

comments.author: Any-Street6992
comments.body: If your stitched curve has a -25% year, that’s good. It means it’s real. Markets are ugly.

comments.author: Suitable-End9642
comments.body:
Fully autonomous for me across multiple instruments — BTC, MNQ (Nasdaq futures), MGC (Micro Gold), and EUR/USD FX.

The core setup runs on Python + ib\_insync against IBKR, no platform in between. Every 15 minutes a strategy agent checks market conditions per instrument — regime (ranging vs trending), ATR, VIX, ES correlation, economic calendar — and either enters, holds, or skips. Trade opens and closes are fully hands off. Telegram sends me a message every time something happens so I always know what the system is doing without being glued to a screen.

The FX side is built differently. EUR/USD runs a dedicated WebSocket price feed writing live bid/ask to a state file, a separate 1-minute bar aggregator, and a scalping strategy that fires every 60 seconds. It only trades during the London/New York overlap window. Whole different architecture from the swing instruments but lives inside the same system and reports to the same Telegram bot.

Every script lives in a private GitHub repo. When I approve a parameter change through the governance layer, it auto-commits with a hash logged to the database so there is a full audit trail of every change that ever went into the system. If something breaks I can roll back to any previous state with one command. That alone has made the whole thing way easier to maintain and iterate on than I expected.

To address the regime shift concern people always raise — I log regime state on every single check, not just on trades. VIX level, ES slope, ATR multiplier, the works. I do not have enough closed trades yet to activate hard regime filters but that data is accumulating. The research loop runs every Sunday and proposes parameter changes based on what actually happened that week. I approve, defer, or reject each one through Telegram buttons. So the system self-tunes but I own every change that goes in.

The part that keeps full autonomy from being reckless is the governance layer. Risk tiers are deterministic — not AI. CRITICAL changes get no approval button, period. HIGH risk changes require a simulation run before I even see an approve option. Economic calendar blocks trading 30 minutes before and after major events like NFP, CPI, FOMC automatically.

AI handles the analysis, nightly summaries, and weekly proposals. I handle framework decisions. That separation is what makes full autonomy actually workable rather than just fast at being wrong.

Total cost to run the whole thing is around $6 to $12 a month. That covers all the AI calls for research, nightly summaries, and governance recommendations. Data feeds for VIX, ES correlation, and economic calendar are all free tier. The FX feed and strategy agents cost nothing extra since they run pure logic with no AI calls. IBKR paper account is free. No platform subscription, no data vendor bill.

Still paper trading while data accumulates. Not going live until the research loop has enough trades to validate the edge properly.

comments.author: flowprompt-ai
comments.body: Probably sitting at 2 trending toward 3 for most serious practitioners. The fully autonomous camp is louder online than it is in actual practice. The honest reality is that regime shifts and liquidity events expose how brittle most autonomous systems are, and the people who have been at this long enough have usually gotten burned at least once by a model that was overfit to a market condition that no longer exists. The hybrid approach is less exciting to talk about but it is where most of the durable results seem to live.

comments.author: 7amed3li
comments.body:
Honestly I think most profitable people are probably using AI more as a research/analysis layer than a “fully autonomous money printer” 😭

The fully autonomous stuff sounds cool until the market regime changes and the model starts confidently doing dumb things with real money.

The most practical use cases I’ve seen are:

* faster backtesting
* feature generation
* pattern/sentiment analysis
* strategy evaluation
* anomaly detection
* summarizing market/news data
* helping build rule-based systems

But risk management usually still ends up being very human-controlled.

A lot of AI trading demos also quietly overfit historical data without surviving real market conditions, slippage, liquidity issues, or news events.

Personally I think the sweet spot right now is:  
AI-assisted research + human-defined risk framework + semi-automated execution.

Basically:  
let AI process more information than you can manually,  
but don’t let it become unsupervised casino mode lol.

comments.author: misscoffeeaddict2
comments.body: i fall firmly into the signals with manual execution category. i have been using signalwhisper for the market intelligence part. it handles the ai-driven chart analysis and gives me clear signals, but i still manage the actual entries and risk. i find that markets are too messy for full autonomy, but having an ai filter the noise saves me hours of manual work.

comments.author: New-Owl5212
comments.body:
Great question — I've been building in this 

space and here's what actually works in practice.



I fall into category 2 and 3 combined:

AI-powered signals with semi-automated execution.



Here's why fully autonomous (category 4) 

is still risky in 2026:

→ AI hallucinates in low-data regimes

→ News spikes need human judgment

→ Risk management still needs human oversight



What's actually working for me:



Built a Gold (XAUUSD) system where:

→ AI analyzes D1 to M1 every morning

→ Gives bias probability with key zones

→ Sends everything to Telegram automatically

→ I make the final execution decision

→ AI then coaches me on the closed trade



So AI handles the heavy analytical work —

pattern recognition, multi-timeframe 

confluence, bias calculation — but human 

makes the final call.



Best of both worlds:

→ Saves 2-3 hours of manual analysis daily

→ Removes emotional bias from analysis

→ Keeps human judgment for execution risk



This hybrid approach has been most 

consistent in my experience.



Happy to share more about the technical 

setup if anyone's curious.
